speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, tape 5. Mr. Zelikson, why don't you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely wife, Janice, my two beautiful daughters, Denise and Linda. They are twins. I also am missing my son, which he passed away, and my older daughter. She lives in New Jersey. She's pregnant, and she's going to have twins. And my other daughter lives in White Plains, and she works downtown.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names, your other two daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helene and Alice. My older daughter is Helene. My other daughter is Alice. And my son was-- his name is Barry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would any of you like to add anything to your father or husband's story, or talk about how this has affected you? Mrs. Zelikson, would you like to start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it hadn't affected me anything. It's just that when we met, you know it was a very-- it was-- I can't explain it. It's-- when we first met, I didn't, you know, I never went out with a European man. And-- and it was very hard because he had also-- the accent was very, you know, different, and I couldn't understand him too well. But my mother and dad took to him likely, and they loved him. He-- he came in the house like he was just-- that they knew him for years. He was very, very warm and loving. And like-- you, you know, he'd just [? say--goes, ?] hi, mom. Hi, dad. He just felt so warm, and he just fitted right in. And I'm very happy we met. And we have a wonderful life together and children. And I didn't let him get away, which he almost did if I didn't run after him. Because he didn't want to tell me about his head. And he thought it would be a burden on me, which, you know, if you love each other, for better, for worse, sickness and-- whatever. And you have to work it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if-- you know, things happen, even if he was fine. And if you live-- you know, things happen in-- in health. But we loved him, and I said, it doesn't matter to me, you know? And he's a good husband. He deserves everything he had. We made him a lovely bar mitzvah, which he never had. And the children and I all chipped in, and we, you know, gave him this honor. And-- When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --he deserve it. May-- May 6 he was bar mitzvahed, this year. And we had a-- a great big affair-- nice. And he deserves every bit of it. I love him dearly. We're married 39 years. And I have lovely children. They're great. They're good. And we had no problem. And we have a lovely life. And we should have 100 more to live and be well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I agree with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's a great man and a great father. No problems. Better thank God we get up in the morning, be healthy and well, and enjoy. Got to enjoy life and be well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Denise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I think that my father's experiences affected me as, I mean, I grew up, and he showed me that you always should have hope. And even when you're in your darkest hour, that you should keep your faith and keep your hope, and that'll get you through life. And I learned to be strong and determined from him. And I-- he brought with him a strong love of his religion and a strong love of God and a faith that, no matter what happens, you always know in your heart that things will be all right. And [LAUGHS] that he taught me to have a strong belief in things. And that to speak up and don't remain silent, and it should never happen again. And that to always be there for the people you love. And that it's more important to have people that love you than anything else in the world. And he was a great father.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a great father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is a great father. And he taught me a lot, not just because he's a father, but because of the person he is. And I admire him very much. And your turn, Linda.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Louder.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was really hard growing up as a child with a father who's a survivor because he kept a lot of things inside. And being so young, you can't really understand what's going on, and you don't understand the enormous impact. And it's really hard, because part of you wants to share it with your father. And the other part is you're really young, and you're scared and you don't understand. And there are things that he kept inside. And only until recently he started really opening up, and really I can understand so much better now. And it helps me to understand people and to understand how I should treat people and how people should treat other people. And all the impact of growing up as a child of a survivor, now that my father is more open about it it's helped me with all the problems or the questions that I had growing up. And he's taught me so much. And that he's taught me even when things are at their worst, you can always be at your best. And he's taught me to be strong and that life is too short and never take anything for granted. And he's also taught me how proud and the meaning to be a Jew. And I never really understood it until I understood his experiences and that, because he was a Jew, what he went through. And recently, it just-- it means so much more now, because I'm able to understand his feelings. And I'm able to understand what it means to really be a Jew and that how many people died because they were Jews. And I think that's the most important thing. And other than that, he's a beautiful and wonderful father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: A great husband, a great dad, and going to be a great grandpa.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure he'll be a wonderful grandfather too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he'll teach them just as he taught his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you. And you have a beautiful family. It was nice meeting you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's me when I was 21 years old, when I came to the United States in New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were taken for a passport, my picture. That's me. That was in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --55. It was also taken for a passport. And my name is Sam. And in Jewish, they used to call me [? Sika. ?] And in Russian, they used to call me [? Shaska. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's my photo. That's me-- the most beautiful photo in my whole life, because at that time I got married to the most beautiful woman in the world. And her name is Janice. My name is Sam. In Jewish, it's [? Sika. ?] In the Russian, it's [? Shaska. ?] And my wife never changed her name. So she's the same. We got married in 1956, June the 16. [INAUDIBLE] We got married in the Bronx in a Jewish synagogue. And at that time, I had a cast on my leg. I broke my leg. You can't see it because they hid it. Excuse me. They hid it very nicely so you can't see. I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Maiden ?] name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Snyder. ?] My wife, before I married her, her name was Janice [? Snyder. ?] Then she became Janice Zelikson. And I'm very, very happy. This is my son-- excuse me-- my son Barry [? Jay, ?] my wonderful son. That-- that picture was taken before he died. That was nine years ago. He was 26 years old. Unfortunate, he died. I miss him very much. I love him very much. And I still see him, and I think he's still helping us. This is me, Sam, [? Sika ?] or [? Shaska, ?] and my adopted brother from [? Canada. ?] We were both [? in ?] the concentration camp, and we survived. And this picture was taken when I became 19-- that was 6-- I became 60 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your friend's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin Kulbak. In Jewish, they call him [? Moisa. ?] K-U-L-B-A-K, Kulbak. That's me, Sam. I was doing my haftarah, the maftir, in Midchester Jewish Center. With my-- with my tallis, I'm reading the Bible. This picture was taken by my bar mitzvah in 1995, May the 6th. This is my entire family, including three cousins from Israel. I'll give you-- from left is my son-in-law Glen Dbrowsky. The second is Helene, my daughter, the oldest daughter, Helene Dbrowsky. The third, of course, is me, Sam, [? Sika ?] or [? Shaska. ?] My other cousin from Israel, his name is Avi Reznik. My wonderful wife, Janice Zelikson. My other cousin from Israel, Erik Reznik. My beautiful daughter, Denise Zelikson. My other cousin is Boris Zelikson. My other [NON-ENGLISH] beautiful daughter, Linda Zelikson. And last, not least, Alice Zelikson, my other daughter. This picture was taken before my bar mitzvah in the Jewish Midchester Center. I'm reading the Bible for my haftarah, and the rabbi is on the left. Also, the reporter wrote the article because he was so excited and [INAUDIBLE] for me, and he couldn't believe my entire story. This was exactly one week before my bar mitzvah in 1995, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end of April. It was April the 30th.
